relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Agrippa permits Paul to speak, and Paul opens his defense.
Paul calls himself blessed to answer before Agrippa.
Agrippa’s knowledge of Judean customs makes patient hearing possible.
Paul’s life among his people is publicly known.
He lived as a Pharisee according to strict ancestral formation.
His trial concerns hope in the ancestral promise.
The twelve tribes serve toward this hope night and day.
The question becomes whether the Divine raises the dead.
Paul once thought opposition to Jesus’ name was necessary.
He imprisoned holy ones and voted against them in death.
Rage drove him to punish and pursue beyond Jerusalem.
This pursuit carried him toward Damascus with priestly authority.
Midday heaven-light brighter than sun surrounded the road.
The fallen pursuer hears his name and the goads.
Jesus names himself as the pursued one.
Paul is raised to become servant and witness.
He is sent to people and nations under divine rescue.
His mission is opened eyes, darkness to light, adversarial authority to Divine, release, inheritance, and sanctified trust.
Paul obeys the heavenly vision.
He proclaims turning, return, and works worthy of turning.
This mission is why he was seized in the temple.
Help from the Divine keeps him witnessing to small and great.
Messiah suffering and resurrection announce light to people and nations.
Festus calls the witness madness.
Paul answers with truth and sober-mindedness.
The king knows; this was not done in a corner.
Paul presses Agrippa toward the prophets.
Agrippa feels the persuasion and names Christian becoming.
Paul wishes all hearers would share his life, except the chains.
The rulers rise from the hearing.
They agree Paul has done nothing death-worthy or bond-worthy.
Agrippa says release would be possible except for the Caesar appeal.
